ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a lemongrass ingredient entry; no dedicated entry for prepared hot lemongrass beverage. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers lemongrass as an ingredient. No specific code exists for the prepared hot beverage form; ingredient-level anchor used as closest defensible match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hot lemongrass beverage or lemongrass drink preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 250 g) of hot lemongrass drink as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre or coffee shop.

nutrient_source.title: Hot Lemongrass — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Hot Lemongrass (cr_id G00011014). All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — herbal and sugar-sweetened beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot lemongrass drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogy with lightly sweetened herbal infusions and sugar-in-water solutions at equivalent sugar concentrations (~4.4g/100g). Sugar dissolved in water at this concentration yields a GI in the 40–55 range; lemongrass fibre and polyphenols may modestly attenuate glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 8.2 g carbohydrate per 100g × 250g serving) / 100 = 9.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar in a sweetened beverage; sodium 145 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request an unsweetened version of this lemongrass drink, as the added sugar (4.4 g/100 g) qualifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage which is associated with elevated blood pressure risk; the sodium content of 145 mg/100 g (362 mg per 250 g serving) also warrants moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 4.4 g sugar per 100 g; rapidly absorbed liquid sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid the sweetened version of this drink because liquid sugar in beverages is absorbed rapidly and raises postprandial blood glucose; requesting an unsweetened preparation eliminates this concern, as lemongrass itself has no adverse glycaemic effect.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; moderate sodium; added sugar present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hot lemongrass drink poses low cardiovascular risk when consumed unsweetened, as it contains negligible saturated fat (HPB data) and zero cholesterol; however, the added-sugar formulation should be avoided or minimised by individuals with cardiovascular disease given the association between sugar-sweetened beverage consumption and adverse lipid profiles.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols in lemongrass offset by pro-inflammatory added sugar
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lemongrass contains citral and polyphenolic compounds with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties, but the added sugar in the sweetened preparation may promote systemic inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version to maximise the anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Lemongrass Drink is a traditional Singaporean herbal beverage prepared by boiling fresh lemongrass stalks (Cymbopogon citratus) in water, typically with pandan leaves and sugar, then straining and serving hot. It is a common offering at Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and traditional herbal drink stalls. The drink has a pale yellow-green colour, a clean citrus-herbal aroma dominated by citral, and a mildly sweet, grassy flavour with a subtle floral note from pandan. Per 100 g, it provides 44 kcal, 1.3 g protein, 1.06 g fat, 8.2 g carbohydrate (4.4 g sugar), 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 145 mg sodium, as recorded in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Lemongrass is rich in citral, limonene, and polyphenolic compounds with documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Clinically, the sweetened version is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension; an unsweetened preparation is suitable for most dietary conditions. The drink is vegan, gluten-free, and halal. It is commonly consumed as a warm digestive or soothing beverage at lunch or dinner.
common_user_queries: Is hot lemongrass drink good for diabetes?
Calories in hot lemongrass drink Singapore
Is lemongrass tea suitable for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Hot Lemongrass (cr_id G00011014) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros are present and internally consistent.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Singapore Foodo source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is expected for a plant-based herbal beverage. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this prepared herbal beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00011014 as of 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 45, low) based on lightly sweetened herbal infusion analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for hot lemongrass drink was found. GL per 250 g serving = (45 × 8.2 × 250) / 10000 = 9.2. The food_name has been expanded to 'Hot Lemongrass Drink' to provide a more canonical and unambiguous English identity while preserving the original HPB name 'Hot Lemongrass' as the primary alias.
